Welcome back. So this fulfillment overview is from a business perspective. So what I just went over was kind of the client outcome, the four pillars that we're doing and why we were doing it. Alright? So from a business standpoint, the revenue and cost units, alright? One coach per 75 clients at an average of $250 a month. And that is averaging together your in-person clients that you may have transitioned over to online or the bolt-on clients that you have added on from your existing business. And the people from Cold Traffic who are coming in on average at $77 a week once you close them on the abandoned Hope sale. Alright? So that is where the $250 number comes from. So at 75 clients, that would be $18,750 a month, which is $225,000 per year per coach in revenue that is being generated from this business. Alright? Now, if we're assuming that we're gonna give a 20%, which you can make a flat fee if you want, you could say it's $50 per customer. If that's easier, if you don't want coaches, you know, counting percentages of what they're making, then it's totally up to you. But I would say roughly 20% is what you should shoot for in terms of compensation. And so this allows them to make $45,000 a year income working from home, not waking up at 4 a.m. Which is a very, very competitive rate for the industry for this type of work. Alright? Now, on top of that, the average client is gonna spend $200 a month on perceived last because that is the basis of your guarantee, right? And so if on average, you have the same 75 clients and they're all buying on average $200 per month in perceived last. Alright? Pure commissions. So, almost $74,000 a year that comes in as extra money. And you'll notice that that is more than the coaches are getting paid. And so the coach can actually pay for him or herself in spades and some by selling products. Okay? Awesome business model. Okay? Now, there are two primary revenue opportunities outside of the primary sale for service. Alright? So they are as follows. First is an institutional orientation. Alright? The goal here is to sell a supplement subscription, and that is inherent in the fact that we have a guarantee. So a service-based guarantee that you're going to continue to service them, provided they stay on this plan. Right? Next, you want to make sure that you're doing these one-on-one. You will make the money back in spades and create a superior onboarding experience. I want to reiterate, this is a hybrid program, so you're not really seeing these people all that often, just two times per month. And so that first experience is really important. Don't try and rush through it. Alright? Like, take your time, give them a great experience, and you will thank me later for it. Alright? I cover the service-based guarantee, but it's based on them using the products as outlined for that correct stage. Alright? If they fail to do that, then your guarantee is void. And so it is up to you to encourage them to make that person's decision for their best interest. Okay? And you can note here that two full-time coaches could do $156,000 a year for you, take-home commissions, and product sales. Alright? Really, really great. Now, the second revenue opportunity is those 14-day-by-weekly check-ins. Alright? So instead of having to necessarily run internal plays, etc., which is, you know, which you can still do, but you wouldn't have to do that because you have a cadence every other week with every customer. And so if you want to start something new or distribute, you know, we have a, there's a new product or new system or a new whatever, you can immediately distribute it into your base of clients, basically every 14 days, without needing to do anything special for it, because you already have their aggregated attention based on the fact they're coming in to meet with you. Alright? So you should see these as selling opportunities from the fact that you're consistently always selling people on the fact that they should stay. Alright? And that's what I'm saying. And so it's great to encourage people because that is a lot of time what they're paying for. Alright? You want to optimize that for the next part of the fulfillment during the check-in so that you can make the prescription that would make sense for that next thing. Alright? In addition to that, you have the pain solution one-offs, which is basically pairing lifestyle strategies with product in order to increase adherence and execution. So an example of that would be somebody who is struggling to get to sleep. Right? Now, you can say, I need you to turn off your phone 30 minutes before bed and you should read stuff. You know, you should you should you should spend have some have some couple time with your spouse, etc. You can say all those things. But if you paired those strategies with the purchase of a product, likely that they do it is much higher. Right? And that's for me anecdotally. I haven't I don't have the research studies because I don't have a prestige labs research study on that specific thing. But you've probably observed that when you give someone a meal plan and they don't pay for it, they probably don't follow it follows the same type of situation. Right? And so if you say, here is the strategy to fix this problem that you're having, you're having trouble getting motivated to even go for a walk. You should try pre-workup and do a single scoop instead of two. You don't mean in that way. You have now given them a means to to to solve the problem, but also monetize the conversation so that the actual time itself is liquidated as well. OK? And so just as a as a fun side note, product sales alone cover all the costs of the coaching overhead plus some. And so if you make sure that your coaches are doing this, then the the commissions earned from this more than cover what you'd actually pay, which means that 100 percent of the service revenue could essentially be in your pocket, not including your rent.
